coredata.dc:title: Facile green and one-pot synthesis of seville orange derived carbon dots as a fluorescent sensor for Fe3+ ions
coredata.dc:creator: Senol, Ayse Merve
Bozkurt, Ebru
coredata.dc:description: In this study, new water-soluble carbon dots (CDs) were fabricated by hydrothermal treatment methods using Seville orange (Citrus aurantium) and ion sensor potential of its was investigated. The as-prepared CDs were characterized morphologically, structurally and optically with spectroscopic techniques. The particle sizes of CDs were determined as average 4.8 nm with TEM images. FTIR, XRD and XPS measurements confirmed the presence of carbon, oxygen and nitrogen atoms and groups such as hydroxyl and carboxyl in the structure of CDs. It was determined that CDs were selective towards Fe3+ ions among different metal ions with fluorescence measurements and limit of detection of Fe3+ ion was found as 0.53 µM. It were revealed by interference studies that no metal ion and anion used affect the Fe3+ ion selectivity of CDs. Real sample tests were performed with tap and ground water samples showed that CDs can be used efficiently for practical applications. All results reflected that these newly synthesized CDs act selectivity and sensitivity in detecting “turn-off” fluorometric Fe3+ ions.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Seville orange
Carbon dots
Fluorometric sensor
Fe3+ ions

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Interference for detection of Fe3+ ion
Detection of Fe3+ ion in water samples
Structural and morphological characterization of CDs

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) UV–Vis. absorption and (b) fluorescence spectra of CDs (λexc = 330 nm).
(a) Fluorescence and (b) normalized fluorescence spectra of CDs at various excitation wavelengths in the range from 300 nm to 510 nm with 10 nm increment.
Fluorescence spectra of CDs in the absence and presence of 0.33 mM metal ions in water (λexc = 330 nm).
(a) The fluorescence responses of the CDs in water after the addition of different concentration of Fe3+ and (b) plot of F0/F with the concentration of Fe3+.
The fluorescence responses of CDs toward the various metal ions in the absence and presence of Fe3+ ions.
Determination of Fe3+ contents in different water samples (n = 3).

head.ce:title: Facile green and one-pot synthesis of seville orange derived carbon dots as a fluorescent sensor for Fe3+ ions

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Faculty of Science, Ataturk University, Erzurum 25240, Turkey
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, Faculty of Science, Ataturk University, Erzurum 25240, Turkey

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Program of Occupational Health and Safety, Vocational College of Technical Sciences, Ataturk University, 25240 Erzurum, Turkey

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Program of Occupational Health and Safety

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Program of Occupational Health and Safety, Vocational College of Technical Sciences, Ataturk University, 25240 Erzurum, Turkey

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this study, new water-soluble carbon dots (CDs) were fabricated by hydrothermal treatment methods using Seville orange (Citrus aurantium) and ion sensor potential of its was investigated. The as-prepared CDs were characterized morphologically, structurally and optically with spectroscopic techniques. The particle sizes of CDs were determined as average 4.8 nm with TEM images. FTIR, XRD and XPS measurements confirmed the presence of carbon, oxygen and nitrogen atoms and groups such as hydroxyl and carboxyl in the structure of CDs. It was determined that CDs were selective towards Fe3+ ions among different metal ions with fluorescence measurements and limit of detection of Fe3+ ion was found as 0.53 µM. It were revealed by interference studies that no metal ion and anion used affect the Fe3+ ion selectivity of CDs. Real sample tests were performed with tap and ground water samples showed that CDs can be used efficiently for practical applications. All results reflected that these newly synthesized CDs act selectivity and sensitivity in detecting “turn-off” fluorometric Fe3+ ions.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots (CDs) has given hope in many potential applications because of their good biocompatibility, chemical inertness, high stability, aqueous solubility and easy functionalization and their excellent optical properties such as tunable emission, stable photoluminescence for the last ten years [1–4]. These potential applications have widely studied in various fields ranging from bio-imaging [5], sensors [6–8] and drug delivery [9] to optoelectronic devices [10], solar cells [11] and photocatalysis [12]. CDs are obtained by several approaches such as chemical ablation [13], electrochemical [14], microwave treatment [15], ultrasonic [16], pyrolisis [17] and hydrothermal treatment methods [18,19]. However, many of these approaches have some disadvantages such as limited spectral efficiency, low product yield, lack of size control, high temperature in experiments, or the use of toxic chemicals. Hydrothermal treatment method has many advantages such as their simplicity of preparation, easy control of reaction, low compustion of energy, low cost and green approach. Also, they generally is not contained toxic chemicals and complex post-production processes. So, great importance has been attached to developing hydrothermal green approaches which is made by using natural sources in recent years [20].

Seville orange (Citrus aurantium) known as bitter orange belonging to the family Rutaceae, is grown world-wide, generally in coastal countries of the Mediterranean Sea and the coasts of Southeast Asia. It has a great importance as a source of edible fruit and a therapeutic in alternative medicine of diseases such as stomachache, vomiting, blood pressure, cough, cold, bronchitis, earache, dysentery, diarrhea, abdominal pain and fever. Until today, the most significant biological active constituents like phenethylamine alkaloids octopamine, synephrine, tyramine, N-methyltyramine and hordenine have been isolated from Seville orange. All of these constituents is rich in carbon and nitrogen sources. Therefore, Seville orange have determined to be quite suitable for carbon dot production. The use of this material for carbon dot synthesis has many advantages such as good water solubility, low cost, less environmental pollution, low toxicity, simple and easy production compared to synthesis with organic molecules [21–25].

Iron, which is a heavy metal, is one of the main causes of water pollution. Therefore, it cause to big problems for human health. Fe3+overloading or deficiency in the human body can caused to various biological disorders, since it plays a role in the enzyme catalysis, cellular metabolism, synthesis and repair of DNA and RNA, oxygen carrrier in hemoglobin etc. Iron accumulation leads to serious diseases such as cancers, Parkinson’s and Alzheimer’s diseases while iron deficiency causes diabetes, anemia, breathing problems, liver, heart and kidney damages. Therefore, it is very important for determination of Fe3+ in the human body or water by using a simple and cheap method. Several methods such as atomic absorption spectrometry, inductively coupled plasma mass spectroscopy, electrochemical methods, and fluorescence spectrometry are used to detect Fe3+. However, these methods are require complex procedures for sample preparation and are quite expensive. Fluorescence spectrometry are more advantageous than other methods because of their advantages such as low-cost, fast response, low limit detection and simplicity [25–29].

In the present study, we reported a new fluorescent CDs to detect Fe3+ ions with high selectivity and sensitivity. These water-soluble CDs were synthesized for the first time from Seville orange by using hydrothermal method without using passivation reagents in a facile, green, economical, and one-pot process. Moreover, this bitter fruit, which is a waste at the roadside in Antalya Manavgat, became a scientifically useful material. The structural ve optical characterizations of CDs were examined by TEM, XRD, FTIR, XPS, absorption and fluorescence spectroscopy. The metal ion selectivity of CDs in aqueous solution were examined by using 16 different metal ions. These CDs in aqueous solution were determined to be high sensitivity and selectivity towards only Fe3+ ions in low detection limit. Therefore, CDs was suggested as a great potential for application in the field of sensing and bio-imaging.

Seville orange (Citrus aurantium) were picked up from Antalya, Manavgat and washed several times with distilled water before preparation of CDs. KCl, ZnCl2, HgCl2, AlCl3, CrCl3·6H2O, FeCl3, MnCl2·4H2O, MgCl2·6H2O, CaCl2·2H2O, CoCl2·6H2O, LiCl, BaCl2·2H2O, AgNO3, CuCl2·2H2O, CdCl2 and FeSO4·7H2O as metal ion source were purchased from Sigma–Aldrich. pH buffer solutions purchased from Merck. All the experiments were performed at room temperature.

The fluorescent CDs were prepared with the following procedure: 50 g of Seville orange were powdered by the assistance of liquid nitrogen (N2). The obtained powders were mixed with 150 mL water. This mixture were transferred into an autoclave and was heated at 130 °C in an over for a period of 12 h. Afterwards, the final mixture was filtered by a 0.22 µm filter membrane and then centrifuged at 13000 rpm for 20 min. The obtained limpid supernatant completed by pure water to 150 mL. The final solution was called a stock solution and its concentration was 40 mg/L at pH 3.34. Desired concentrations of the CDs were obtained by dilution of the stock solution. 5 mg/L of CDs in aqueous solution was used for its spectroscopic characterization.

Structural, morphological and optical properties of CDs were investigated by Transmission electron microscope (TEM), Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR), X-ray diffractometry (XRD), X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), UV–vis. spectroscopy and steady state fluorescence spectroscopy. XRD patterns of the samples were collected by using a PANalytical’s X-ray diffractometers. TEM images were recorded on a FEI TALOS F200S TEM 200 kV. FTIR measurements were done using a Bruker VERTEX 70v, in the wavenumber range of 1200–4000 cm−1. The surface chemical composition was investigated by XPS using Spect‐Flex with a standard Al X‐ray source. Inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometry (ICP-MS) Agilent 7700 was used for a control assay in the water samples. The UV–vis. absorption and fluorescence spectra of the samples were recorded using Shimadzu UV-3600 Plus UV–VIS-NIR Spectrophotometer and Agilent Technologies Cary Eclipse Fluorescence Spectrophotometer, respectively.

Fluorescence quantum yields (Φf) of the CDs samples were calculated by making comparison with a reference dye, Quinine sulphate (Φf = 0.546 in 0.05 M H2SO4). The method applied for the determination of fluorescence quantum yields were described in elsewhere [29–31].

CDs solution with a concentration of 5 mg/L were prepared by diluting with pure water from CDs stock solution. Afterwards, the different concentrations of cations was separately added to CDs solution. The as-prepared solutions were thoroughly mixed at room temperature. Then, the absorption and fluorescence measuruments were recorded for each sample.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Interference for detection of Fe3+ ion
ce:section.ce:para: The fluorescence response in the region of 330 nm of the mixture of CDs + Fe3+ (0.33 mM) was examined separately in the presence of 0.33 mM other metal ions (K+, Li+, Ag+, Ba2+, Ca2+, Mg2+, Cd2+, Co2+, Fe2+, Cu2+, Hg2+, Mn2+ Zn2+, Al3+, and Cr3+) in the room temperature.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of Fe3+ ion in water samples
ce:section.ce:para: Real sample tests were examined for evaluating the potential application performance of CDs in tap water and ground water samples. ICP-MS measurements were taken to know the amount of Fe3+ in the tap water and ground water samples. Three different samples were prepared by adding Fe3+ in different concentrations for tap water and ground water and then these samples were taken fluorescence measurements.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Structural and morphological characterization of CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The water-soluble CDs were simply prepared by facile hydrothermal treatment of Seville orange at room temperature. The morphology and surface functional groups of CDs were characterized by TEM, FTIR, XRD and XPS. Fig. S1a and S1b showed representative TEM images and particle size distribution histogram of CDs with average particle sizes of 4.8 nm. It was clear that CDs with a narrow size distribution ranging from 4 nm to 6 nm have monodispers distribution and spherical shapes.

The functional groups on the CDs surface was determined by FTIR spectrum (Fig. S2). The peak associated with the stretching vibrations of carbonyl groups (CO) and aromatic CN heterocycles were observed at 1714 cm−1 and 1604 cm−1, respectively. The peaks at 2905 cm−1and 3346 cm−1 were assigned to the stretching vibrations of C–H and O–H groups. The band at 1378 cm−1 was due to O–H deformations of the C-OH groups [31–33]. CDs were hydrophilic and stable in aqueous solution as a result of their functional groups containing oxygen. The corresponding XRD pattern, shown in Fig S3, reflected a broad peak centered 2θ ~ 24°, which was indicated amorphous carbon ordered in a considerably random fashion [16,34].

The XPS measurements were also performed to make further elemental characterization of CDs. The XPS spectrum showed three strong peaks at 531.0, 400.8, and 284.5 eV, which are attributed to O1s, N1s, and C1s, respectively (Fig. S4a). Specifically, the deconvolution of the C1s spectrum (Fig. S4b) exhibited three peaks at 288.0, 285.8 and 284.6 eV, which were assigned to the CO, C–N and sp2 C–C groups, respectively. The O1s spectrum (Fig. S4c) had two peaks at 532.4 and 530.8 eV, which were attributed to the C–OH/C–O–C and CO groups, respectively. The two peaks at 401.1 and 399.5 eV in N1s spectrum (Fig. S4d) were come from H2N-C and C–N–C groups, respectively [32,35]. The data obtained from the XPS spectrum were highly compatible with the FTIR spectrum.

CDs were optically characterized by absorption and fluorescence measurements. The solution of CDs was light yellow in daylight and bright blue under UV light. The absorption and fluorescence spectra of CDs solution were given in Fig. 1 a and b. As seen Fig. 1a, the absorption of CDs was exhibited a peak at nearly 283 nm, which ascribed to π–π* transition of aromatic CC bonds. The CDs displayed an intense fluorescence band at 402 nm, when excited at 330 nm. The effect of excitation wavelength on fluorescence of CDs was also investigated. The fluorescence band maxima of CDs were observed to be dependent on the excitation wavelength (Fig. 2 a). The fluorescence spectra of CDs were taken by changing the excitation wavelength between 300 and 510 nm at 10 nm intervals. As the excitation wavelength increased, the fluorescence band maximum of CDs shifted from 401 nm to 540 nm (Fig. 2b). The fluorescence intensity of as-prepared CDs depended on the excitation wavelength was shown in Fig. 2a. The fluorescence intensity of CDs increased up to 330 nm and then it decreased. It is known that the fluorescence intensity of carbon-based nanomaterials changes depending on the excitation wavelength due to the optical selection of particles with different sizes and the surface defects of the particles [2]. Furthermore, the fluorescence quantum yield (QY) of CDs was calculated to be 13.3%, by using quinine sulfate as a reference. The effect of pH on the optical properties of CDs were also investigated by using absorption and fluorescence measuruments and the results were summarized in the Supplementary file.

In order to understand the binding properties of CDs with 16 metal ions, we were tested the fluorescence responses of CDs by the addition of the metal ions including K+, Li+, Ag+, Ba2+, Ca2+, Mg2+, Cd2+, Co2+, Fe2+, Cu2+, Hg2+, Mn2+ Zn2+, Fe3+, Al3+, and Cr3+. For this purpose, the fluorescence spectra of CDs were taken in the presence and absence of selected ions (Fig. 3 ). It was observed that the fluorescence intensity of CDs did not change in the presence of other ions except for Fe3+ ions, but the fluorescence intensity of CDs decreased significantly in the presence of Fe3+ ions. Among the 16 different ions, only Fe3+ showed higher affinity with the nitrogen and oxygen atom in CDs, and coordination between CDs and Fe3+ ions occurred. Thus, the fluorescence of CDs was quenched due to electron transfer from CDs to Fe3+ ions. This showed that newly synthesized CDs were high selective for Fe3+ ions. Furthermore, the effect of Fe3+ ions on the absorption of CDs were determined (Fig. S7). The data obtained in the absorption spectra were quite consistent with the data obtained in the fluorescence spectra.

The fluorescence measurements for CDs solutions were taken to determine the sensitivity of this system at different concentrations of Fe3+ (Fig. 4 a, b and S8). The fluorescence intensity of CDs decreased with increasing Fe3+concentration (Fig. 4a). This change showed the sensitivity of CDs to Fe3+ ions. The quenching efficiency was analyzed with the Stern-Volmer equation. This equation;
(1)
F 0
F
= 1 +
K
SV
[ Q ]
where KSV is the Stern–Volmer quenching constant, [Q] is the concentration of Fe3+,and F0 and F are the fluorescence intensities at 407 nm in the absence and presence of Fe3+ ions, respectively [36]. Fig. 4b showed a good linearity in the range of 0.033 mM–0.133 mM (R2 = 0.99). KSV was found as 1.621 × 103 µM−1 from the slope of Fig. 4b. The detection limit (LOD) was determined from 3 s/k equation, where s was the standard deviation from the blank sample and k was slope of Fig. 4b. LOD was found to be 0.53 µM. This value was quite low compared to other carbon dot sensors in the literature [37,38].

Selectivity is an important parameter in determining the detection ability of a sensor [39]. Fe3+ ion selectivity of CDs was investigated in the presence of other metal ions (K+, Li+, Ag+, Ba2+, Ca2+, Mg2+, Cd2+, Co2+, Fe2+, Cu2+, Hg2+, Mn2+ Zn2+, Al3+, and Cr3+). Each metal ion was added to CDs solution containing Fe3+ ions separately and the fluorescence measurements of these solutions were taken at room temperature. The results showed that none of the competing ions affected Fe3+ ion selectivity of CDs (Fig. 5 ). Fe3+ ion selectivity of the newly synthesized CDs was also tested in the presence of various anions (F−, Cl−, Br−, I−, NO3 −, SCN−, CN−, CH3COO−, S2−, CO3 2−, SO4 2−, C2O4 2−, and PO4 3−). It was observed that the presence of any of these thirteen different anions in the solutions did not affect the selectivity of CDs towards Fe3+ ions (Fig. S10). As a result of these observations, it was suggested that this new CDs are very good sensor system for the detection of Fe3+ ions selectively without interfering with the other ions.

The usability of CDs in real samples for Fe3+ ions detection was explored with various water samples (tap and ground water). The tap and ground water samples were analyzed by both ICP-MS and the used sensing technique to validate the reliability of the sensor. Neither our sensing materials nor the ICP-MS-based system detected the presence of Fe3+ ions in the water samples. Then, different concentrations of Fe3+ ions (10, 20 and 30 µM) were added to the water samples and the samples were analyzed. The obtained results were summarized in Table 1 . The recoveries ranged from 93.6 to 100% with relative standard deviations of less than 5.0% (n = 3). These results showed that new CDs can be used in practical applications.

Highly fluorescent novel carbon dots were synthesized by hydrothermal treatment methods using seville orange and they were characterized structurally, morphologically and optically by various spectroscopic techniques. A new sensor for the determination of Fe3+ ions was developed based on fluorescence quenching. The usability of CDs in practical applications was tested in tap and ground water samples. In summary, we suggested that these new CDs, which were synthesized in a very simple way, could be used as a “turn-off” fluorometric sensor for Fe3+ ions detection.
Ayse Merve Senol: Visualization, Methodology, Writing. Ebru Bozkurt: Data curation, Visualization, Conceptualization, Investigation, Methodology, Writing - review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.microc.2020.105357.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1
